trajectory_summary: This is a warm-carry call: a cooperative, already-interested customer sustained a procedurally adequate but emotionally flat rep to a booked sit. Randy completed the structural beats — verify, credit qualifier, verbal bill readback (correctly executed per the elderly_low_tech_protocol), and a partial button-up — but left two bill-pain flip opportunities on the table and failed to capture Susan's stated install preference for the handoff. The appointment is real and the customer is genuine; the quality of the sit depends on whether the closer walks in knowing what Susan actually told the setter.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ends with a confirmed appointment for the following day at 2PM. The outcome should be tagged as 'appointment_booked.' The booking is structurally sound — Randy correctly used verbal bill readback when Susan indicated she was not comfortable with phone photography, confirmed the time, and stated the one-hour block. However, two button-up elements are missing (phone_appointment_specified and is_that_fair_handshake), and the closer will not know Susan's barn-install preference unless the handoff brief captures it. The sit is real but the handoff is incomplete.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I don't use my air conditioner. We haven't used it for over twenty years because it would just be too expensive.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Susan hands Randy the emotional center of the call twice — once at the open (bill went up) and once unprompted during bill readback (twenty years without AC due to cost). Both times Randy acknowledges and moves on. This is the schema's universal floor failure: bill capture without the flip. The second instance is the sharper coaching moment because Susan is volunteering a twenty-year behavioral adaptation to bill pain — the exact leverage the objection_inversion move is built for. Randy has the information; he does not use it. This is the single highest-value coaching beat for this rep on this call.

key_moments.quote: Oh, wow. That's that's awesome. That's actually really good.
key_moments.stewart_read: Randy captures the $57 bill figure and responds with genuine enthusiasm — but the enthusiasm is directed at the number, not at Susan's pain. The schema is explicit: capture alone is not sufficient. The move is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' Randy does ask and acknowledge; the flip never comes. The $57 budget-plan figure is actually a useful anchor: Susan is on a managed plan precisely because her real usage would cost more. That gap between the budget number and her actual consumption is the flip opportunity — but Randy doesn't take it.

key_moments.quote: I don't use my air conditioner. We haven't used it for over twenty years because it would just be too expensive.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the sharpest bill-pain signal in the call — and it arrives unprompted, mid-bill-readback, as Susan is reading off kWh numbers. She has been self-rationing electricity for two decades because of cost. This is not a casual aside; it is a twenty-year behavioral adaptation to bill pain. Randy responds 'Yeah. Yeah. For sure. Oh, that definitely makes sense.' and moves to scheduling. The flip here is not about the $57 number — it is about the twenty years of discomfort. The leverage is the cost of staying, not the cost of switching.

key_moments.quote: Yes. And I would like to put the solar panel from the barn, because we do use it's actually the shop for equipment.
key_moments.stewart_read: Susan states a clear install preference — the barn, not the house — and gives a functional reason (equipment shop, active electricity use). Randy acknowledges it ('Okay. Gotcha. Does the barn have its own meter?') and notes it looks like a great spot for solar, but he does not flag this preference for the design team or the closer. The schema's cross_sell_signals section does not yet cover secondary-structure solar (explicitly TBD), so this is not a routing failure. It is a handoff_brief_discipline failure: the closer will receive a design built for the house roof unless this preference is explicitly passed forward.

key_moments.quote: Yes. Yeah. And I had put it because I just live across the street from the post office, and that's where I get my mail. They don't give us nickel. It's where I live, so I have to go across the street.
key_moments.stewart_read: Susan is explaining that her mailing address (438 Jerales Road) is a post office box across the street from her physical residence. Randy treats this as small talk ('Oh, that's nice. That sounds convenient.') and moves on. The physical address ambiguity has direct implications: the design team is pulling Google Maps imagery of the mailing address, not necessarily the property where the panels will be installed. If the two addresses differ, the satellite design will be built on the wrong structure.
